title: Matrescence
subtitle: A reading on the becoming-a-mother that runs alongside the new baby, often unnamed
excerpt: A ten-minute reading on matrescence: the becoming-a-mother that runs alongside the new baby. Named in 1973 by anthropologist Dana Raphael; named again by Dr Aurélie Athan for the modern reader. The piece that nobody told you about.
sourceNotes: Original to homemade.education. The term matrescence was coined by anthropologist Dana Raphael (1973) and brought back into wider use by Dr Aurélie Athan, a clinical psychologist at Columbia University.

content.text: There is a word for the change that happens when a person becomes a mother. The word is matrescence. It is the word for the becoming, not the baby.

Adolescence is the same shape. The body changes. The brain changes. The hormones change. The way the person fits into the world changes. The old self is left behind and a new one is being built, slowly, in real time.
The word came from the anthropologist Dana Raphael in 1973. She used it to name what she saw in the women she studied. It was a stage as real as adolescence, with as little support.

The word sat quietly in academic writing for forty years. It came back through the work of Dr Aurélie Athan at Columbia University. She taught a generation of new mothers that there was a word for what was happening to them.
Everything. The hours of the day. The body. The hormones. The friendships. The work. The marriage. The sense of who you are when the room is quiet.
The brain changes too. Grey matter shifts in regions linked to social processing. The change holds for years after the baby. It is not a flaw; it is a re-wiring for the new role.

The parts of you that were there before the baby do not go away. They go underground for a while. The reader, the friend, the cook, the woman who loved a long bath, the woman with a sharp opinion about the news. They are waiting.
Matrescence is not a deletion of the old self. It is an integration. The new self holds the old one inside it.
Naming it is the work. Once the change has a name, the disorientation has a frame. The crying for no reason has a frame. The grief for the old self has a frame. The wonder at the baby has a frame.
Without the word, the mother thinks she is failing at being herself. With the word, she knows she is in a passage that takes years.

Adolescence takes about a decade. Matrescence is the same. The first three years are the loudest. The next seven are the settling. By year ten, the new self is the steady self. The woman who started the passage has changed shape entirely.

Original to homemade.education. The term matrescence was coined by anthropologist Dana Raphael in 1973. It was brought back into wider use by Dr Aurélie Athan of Columbia University. The brain-change research draws on the work of Elseline Hoekzema and others (2017 onwards) on the structural changes in the maternal brain.
